response.response_key_js: (function(){ var img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img'); if (!img) return null; var src = img.src || ''; var isCircle = src.indexOf('circle') !== -1; var groupIdx = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index !== 'undefined' && window.efVars.group_index !== null) ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; var circleIsComma = (groupIdx >= 0 && groupIdx <= 4); if (isCircle) return circleIsComma ? ',' : '.'; return circleIsComma ? '.' : ','; })()

phase_detection.feedback: document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus') === null && document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus') === null && document.querySelector('#jspsych-instructions-next') === null && document.querySelector('#jspsych-fullscreen-btn') === null && (((document.body && document.body.innerText) || '').toLowerCase().indexOf('press') !== -1) && (((document.body && document.body.innerText) || '').toLowerCase().indexOf('enter') !== -1)

attention_check.response_js: (function(){ var stimEl = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!stimEl) return null; var text = stimEl.innerText || stimEl.textContent || ''; var m = text.match(/Press the (\w) key/i); if (m) return m[1].toLowerCase(); if (/third to last letter/i.test(text)) return 'x'; if (/second to last letter/i.test(text)) return 'y'; if (/last letter/i.test(text) && !/to last/i.test(text)) return 'z'; if (/first letter/i.test(text)) return 'a'; if (/second letter/i.test(text) && !/to last/i.test(text)) return 'b'; if (/third letter/i.test(text) && !/to last/i.test(text)) return 'c'; return 'a'; })()

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., Beste, C., Bissett, P. G., Brockett, A. T., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean go-trial RT in healthy adults typically falls between ~500-600 ms with SD ~100-140 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to go RT typically yield mu in the 400-500 ms range under choice stop-signal conditions.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.table_or_figure: Tables of empirical mu/sigma/tau across choice-RT studies
citations.quote: mu values in two-choice tasks cluster around 400-500 ms across reviewed datasets.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: sigma values in choice RT typically span ~30-80 ms across studies.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma was estimated in the 40-70 ms range across conditions.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Tau values typically range from ~60-150 ms in two-choice paradigms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Tau on go trials in stop-signal task averages ~80-130 ms.

go.rationale: Healthy-adult go-trial RT distributions in the stop-signal task are typically reported with mean ~550-580 ms, SD ~110-140 ms, and a positive skew driven by an exponential tail of ~80-120 ms. Mu=460 places the Gaussian center slightly below the mean to leave room for the tau component (mean = mu + tau ≈ 560 ms). Sigma=60 reflects within-condition Gaussian variability of choice-RT under stop-signal anticipation (somewhat wider than pure choice-RT due to proactive slowing). Tau=100 is at the center of the published range for go-RT skew in stop-signal paradigms with adaptive SSD.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs are drawn from the lower portion of the go-RT distribution and are systematically faster than mean go-RT.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Failed-stop (signal-respond) RT should be shorter than mean go RT; this is a defining race-model prediction.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Signal-respond distributions are truncated tails of the go distribution and thus narrower in spread.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Sigma on signal-respond trials is comparable to or slightly smaller than go-trial sigma.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Successful inhibition truncates the slow tail of the go distribution; signal-respond RTs therefore have reduced positive skew.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Tau on signal-respond trials is reduced relative to go-trial tau, consistent with race-model truncation.

stop_signal.rationale: On failed-inhibition (signal-respond) trials, RTs are systematically faster than mean go-RT (a key prediction of the Logan-Cowan race model: signal-respond RTs come from the lower tail of the go-RT distribution). Published failed-stop RTs run ~60-100 ms below mean go-RT. Mu=380 (mean ≈ 460 ms) yields the expected leftward shift; sigma and tau are slightly compressed because the race model truncates the slowest go responses (which would have been successfully inhibited).

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations in trial-by-trial RT series typically lie in the 0.1-0.25 range.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Fluctuations in the time required for elementary decisions
citations.quote: Speeded RT exhibits long-range temporal dependence with positive low-lag autocorrelation.

autocorrelation.rationale: Lag-1 autocorrelation of ~0.10-0.20 is a robust universal feature of trial-by-trial RT series in speeded-choice tasks, including stop-signal. Phi=0.15 is the central published estimate.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Creatures of habit (and control): a multi-level learning perspective on the modulation of congruency effects
citations.quote: Stimulus/response repetition effects (Gratton-style) are characteristic of conflict paradigms (flanker/Stroop) but are not a defining feature of the stop-signal task.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Creatures of habit (and control): a multi-level learning perspective on the modulation of congruency effects
citations.quote: Sequential conflict adaptation effects are not part of the canonical stop-signal phenomenology; stop-after-stop sequence effects are subsumed under post-interrupt slowing.

condition_repetition.rationale: Condition-repetition (Gratton-style sequence) effects are canonical for conflict paradigms (flanker/Stroop) but are not a defining feature of the stop-signal paradigm. Stop-after-stop sequence effects exist but are subsumed under post_interrupt_slowing; enabling both would double-count.

citations.authors: Lim, J., Wu, W. C., Wang, J., Detre, J. A., Dinges, D. F., & Rao, H.
citations.title: Imaging brain fatigue from sustained mental workload
citations.quote: Sustained-attention RT slows progressively across blocks of speeded trials.

citations.authors: Csathó, Á., van der Linden, D., Hernádi, I., Buzás, P., & Kalmár, G.
citations.title: Effects of mental fatigue on the capacity limits of visual attention
citations.quote: Time-on-task slowing on the order of tens of milliseconds across ~30-min sessions is reliably observed.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Modest time-on-task slowing of ~0.1-0.3 ms/trial is consistently observed across speeded RT tasks of ~150-200 trials. Stop-signal sessions of this length show small but reliable mean-RT drifts.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: 1/f scaling residuals around the mean RT typically have amplitudes of 20-30 ms in choice tasks.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Long-range temporal residuals in RT series have SDs in the tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Hurst exponents for cognitive RT series center near 0.7-0.8, consistent with persistent long-range correlation.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Estimated Hurst exponents in cognitive tasks lie in the 0.65-0.85 range.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f^alpha (pink-noise) structure in RT series is a universal property of human trial-level performance (Gilden and colleagues). Hurst ~0.7-0.8 and SD ~20-30 ms reflect typical scaling in speeded-choice RT including stop-signal go-trials.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RT on the trial following an error is reliably slower than RT following a correct trial.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing in choice RT typically falls between ~10 and 50 ms depending on measurement method.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Upper-end PES estimates reach 40-60 ms in many published datasets.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes commonly range from ~20 to 50 ms.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing on the trial after a choice error is well documented in stop-signal go-trials (typical magnitude ~20-40 ms). The range covers individual differences while remaining within the meta-analytic central tendency.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go-RT after a stop trial is reliably slower than go-RT after a go trial, regardless of inhibition success.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Post-stop slowing on subsequent go trials is one of the most robust sequential phenomena in the paradigm.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Post-stop slowing magnitudes typically span ~20-70 ms across blocks and participants.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., & Stevens, M. A.
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Sequential post-stop slowing is reliably observed in stop-signal datasets collected with this protocol.

post_interrupt_slowing.rationale: Post-stop slowing (go-RT after a stop-signal trial relative to go-after-go) is one of the most reliably documented sequential effects in the stop-signal paradigm, with typical magnitudes of 20-60 ms regardless of inhibition success. This is paradigm-specific and empirically essential.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD in mean go-RT in healthy-adult stop-signal samples is ~80-110 ms; rt_mean_sd_ms=90 sits at the center. Condition-specific RT differences (go minus failed-stop) vary across subjects with SD ~20-30 ms. Ex-Gaussian sigma and tau show roughly 30-40% multiplicative individual variability, captured by the [0.7, 1.4] range. Go-trial accuracy in healthy adults clusters near ceiling (~0.95) with SD ~0.03; omission rates vary modestly (~0.02 SD) reflecting attention/engagement differences.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean go-RT in healthy adult samples typically lies between 80 and 110 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs in mean RT for stop-signal and similar tasks are reported on the order of 80-100 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs of condition difference scores in stop-signal-style tasks fall in the ~20-40 ms range.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Individual differences in (mean go-RT minus failed-stop RT) typically have SDs of ~20-30 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.table_or_figure: Distributional summaries of mu/sigma/tau across studies
citations.quote: Tau and sigma show substantial multiplicative individual variability, often spanning roughly 0.5x-1.5x the group mean.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Love, J., & Heathcote, A.
citations.title: A Bayesian approach for estimating the probability of trigger failures in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Individual posteriors for sigma and tau exhibit ~30-40% multiplicative variability around group means.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Healthy-adult go-trial accuracy clusters near ceiling (~0.94-0.98) with SD ~0.02-0.04.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Accuracy variance in well-practiced choice-RT tasks is small in healthy adults, with SDs near 0.03.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Aron, A. R., Band, G. P. H., et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Go-trial omission rates in healthy adults are typically <5% with between-subject SD of ~0.02-0.03.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stop_signal_rdoc' with 3 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 5 temporal effects.
Produced 40 citations across 21 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 27 parameters; 4 are high-sensitivity.
